serving.natural_language_portion: A typical side dish serving of stir-fried nai bai in a Singaporean meal is approximately 150 g, equivalent to a small bowl or side plate portion.

nutrient_source.title: Stir Fried Nai Bai — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo entry for Stir Fried Nai Bai (F14080053). All core macros and secondary nutrients are sourced from this record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for stir-fried nai bai. GI estimated at 15 based on analogous non-starchy leafy green vegetables (e.g., bok choy, Chinese cabbage) from the University of Sydney GI Database, which consistently report GI values in the 10–20 range. Glycemic load per 150 g serving calculated as: (15 × 2.4 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 1.5) / 100 = 0.54, rounded to 0.5.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 338 mg per 100 g from soy sauce and oyster sauce seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Sodium at 338 mg/100 g is in the borderline-elevated range; individuals with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce and oyster sauce to lower sodium content, while benefiting from nai bai's naturally occurring potassium (~200 mg/100 g) and calcium (~80 mg/100 g) which support blood pressure regulation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate (2.4 g/100 g) and sugar (0.7 g/100 g) with estimated GI of 15
type2_diabetes.guidance: Stir-fried nai bai is an excellent choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very low carbohydrate content (2.4 g/100 g), negligible sugar (0.7 g/100 g), and estimated GI of 15, resulting in a glycemic load of only 0.5 per serving, with minimal impact on postprandial blood glucose.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (~0.3 g/100 g), no trans fat, and high dietary fibre (1.57 g/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Stir-fried nai bai is cardioprotective due to its very low saturated fat (~0.3 g/100 g), absence of trans fat, and meaningful dietary fibre (1.57 g/100 g); however, the sodium content of 338 mg/100 g warrants moderation in seasoning for individuals managing cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory micronutrients including vitamin C (~25 mg/100 g) and calcium (~80 mg/100 g)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Nai bai provides vitamin C (~25 mg/100 g) as an antioxidant supporting joint health and calcium (~80 mg/100 g) for bone protection; saturated fat is negligible (~0.3 g/100 g) and trans fat is absent, making this a suitable dish for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis, though sodium at 338 mg/100 g should be minimised to reduce inflammatory tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stir-fried nai bai is a popular Singaporean Chinese vegetable side dish made from nai bai (奶白菜, milk cabbage), a tender leafy green from the Brassicaceae family closely related to bok choy. The vegetable is stir-fried at high heat in a wok with garlic, cooking oil, and seasoning typically including soy sauce and oyster sauce until the leaves are wilted and fragrant. The result is a lightly sauced, tender-crisp dish with a mild, slightly sweet flavour and a soft texture. Nutritionally, it is very low in calories (37 kcal/100 g), low in carbohydrates (2.4 g/100 g), and provides modest protein (1.95 g/100 g) and fat (2.25 g/100 g). It is a good source of dietary fibre (1.57 g/100 g), vitamin C (~25 mg/100 g), calcium (~80 mg/100 g), and potassium (~200 mg/100 g). The sodium content of 338 mg/100 g reflects the soy and oyster sauce seasoning and is the primary clinical consideration. With an estimated glycemic index of 15 and glycemic load of 0.5 per serving, it is highly suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. It is commonly served as a side dish in hawker centres, zi char restaurants, and home cooking across Singapore, and is suitable for vegetarian diets when oyster sauce is substituted with vegetarian oyster sauce.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry F14080053 with confidence 0.95. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database for non-starchy leafy greens. Saturated fat, potassium, calcium, and vitamin C values are consistent with published USDA FoodData Central data for comparable cooked Chinese cabbage/bok choy preparations.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for stir-fried nai bai is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry F14080053. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in the HPB source. iron_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: micronutrient detail not available in the HPB Foodo entry for this dish. saturated_fat_g (~0.3 g/100 g), potassium_mg (~200 mg/100 g), calcium_mg (~80 mg/100 g), and vitamin_c_mg (~25 mg/100 g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for cooked bok choy / Chinese cabbage (USDA NDB 11117) as the closest approved-source analogue; these are approximate values. trans_fat_g set to 0.0: confirmed negligible for a vegetable stir-fry with standard cooking oil. cholesterol_mg set to 0.0: plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients contributing cholesterol. vitamin_b12_mcg set to 0.0: plant-based dish; B12 is naturally absent. vitamin_d_mcg set to 0.0: negligible in leafy green vegetables. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate based on non-starchy leafy green vegetables from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for stir-fried nai bai was found in approved sources. Dietary tag 'keto' from input retained as defensible given carbohydrate content of 2.4 g/100 g. Vegan flag set to false as standard preparation includes oyster sauce; vegetarian flag set to true as the dish is commonly prepared without meat.
